Deep Neural Networks (DNN) are used in model distillation studies to condense the scale of models by distilling knowledge from a teacher model into a compact student one, indicating a relationship between DNN and model distillation.

Model distillation studies have led to the consideration of dataset distillation, which aims to condense the size of real-world datasets, suggesting a relationship between model distillation and dataset distillation.

The authors Wang et al. were the first to formally study dataset distillation, proposing a method that models a regular optimizer and uses an additional optimizer to update the synthetic dataset pixel-by-pixel, linking dataset distillation to the work by Wang et al.

The synthetic dataset optimized by gradient-matching methods is mentioned in the context. The dataset is described as generalizing to various starting weights but not robust enough to mitigate the perturbation caused by accumulated trajectory error.

Robust learning is suggested as an approach to minimize error in the system by intentionally adding perturbations to the starting weights. This approach aims to make the system more resilient to errors.
Robust learning adds perturbations to the starting weights intentionally during training to make the system robust to errors.
Robust learning increases the amount of information of the real dataset to be distilled.
Distillation from the increased information results in convergence issues and degrades the final performance.

Dataset Distillation involves synthesizing a new dataset (S) much smaller than the original real dataset (T).
The goal of Dataset Distillation is to train a model on the synthetic dataset (S) to achieve comparable performance to the model trained on the original dataset (T).

The FTD approach proposes to regularize the teacher trajectory to a flat trajectory for distillation, aiming to distill a synthetic dataset with a flat standard training trajectory that is robust to weight perturbations.

FTD improves the buffer phase to make the teacher trajectory robust to weight perturbation, guiding the distillation gradient update to synthesize a dataset with a flat trajectory characteristic in a standard optimization procedure.

The optimization of teacher trajectories in existing gradient-matching methods is simplified to improve convergence of distillation. The accumulation of trajectory error may be a reason to limit the total number of training epochs of teacher trajectories, calling for further research.
